facts.text: Isaac Asimov published over 500 books in his lifetime, spanning science fiction, mystery, and popular science.
The term 'science fiction' was popularized by Hugo Gernsback in the late 1920s, though he initially called it 'scientifiction.'
Jules Verne's 1870 novel 'Twenty Thousand Leagues Under the Sea' predicted the development of electrically powered submarines.
Arthur C. Clarke proposed the concept of geostationary communication satellites in a 1945 paper, decades before they became reality.
Frank Herbert's 'Dune' was rejected by over 20 publishers before being accepted by Chilton, a company known for publishing auto repair manuals.
The Hugo Award for science fiction is named after Hugo Gernsback, the founder of 'Amazing Stories' magazine.
Ray Bradbury wrote the first draft of 'Fahrenheit 451' on a coin-operated typewriter in the UCLA library basement.
Philip K. Dick's novel 'Do Androids Dream of Electric Sheep?' explored the philosophical question of what makes someone human.
H.G. Wells published 'The Time Machine' in 1895, popularizing the concept of mechanical time travel in fiction.
The Fermi Paradox asks why, given the high probability of extraterrestrial civilizations, we have found no evidence of them.
William Gibson coined the term 'cyberspace' in his 1982 short story 'Burning Chrome,' two years before using it in 'Neuromancer.'
Ursula K. Le Guin's 'The Left Hand of Darkness' explored a society where individuals have no fixed gender.
Isaac Asimov formulated the Three Laws of Robotics in his 1942 short story 'Runaround.'
The Drake Equation estimates the number of active communicative extraterrestrial civilizations in the Milky Way galaxy.
Neal Stephenson's 1992 novel 'Snow Crash' introduced the concept of the 'Metaverse,' a virtual reality-based successor to the internet.
Robert Heinlein's 1961 novel 'Stranger in a Strange Land' introduced the word 'grok' into the English language.
A Dyson sphere is a hypothetical megastructure that completely encompasses a star to capture its energy output.
N.K. Jemisin became the first author to win the Hugo Award for Best Novel three years in a row, for her Broken Earth trilogy.
The concept of a space elevator was first proposed by Konstantin Tsiolkovsky in 1895, inspired by the Eiffel Tower.
Edward Bellamy's 1888 novel 'Looking Backward' predicted the widespread use of credit cards over a century before they became common.
Liu Cixin's 'The Three-Body Problem' became the first Asian novel to win the Hugo Award for Best Novel in 2015.
The Turing test, proposed by Alan Turing in 1950, measures a machine's ability to exhibit intelligent behavior indistinguishable from a human.
Aldous Huxley's 'Brave New World' predicted the use of antidepressant drugs, genetic engineering, and reproductive technology.
WorldCon, the World Science Fiction Convention, has been held annually since 1939 and is the venue for the Hugo Awards.
The manga 'Akira' by Katsuhiro Otomo is set in a post-apocalyptic Neo-Tokyo in the year 2019.
Arthur C. Clarke's 'Profiles of the Future' established Clarke's Three Laws, the most famous being 'Any sufficiently advanced technology is indistinguishable from magic.'
Philip K. Dick experienced what he believed were mystical visions in 1974, which he spent the rest of his life trying to understand.
The Nebula Award has been given by the Science Fiction and Fantasy Writers of America since 1966.
Jules Verne's 'From the Earth to the Moon' (1865) accurately predicted that the American space launch site would be in Florida.
Ted Chiang's short story 'Story of Your Life' explores the Sapir-Whorf hypothesis through contact with an alien species.
H.G. Wells predicted the atomic bomb in his 1914 novel 'The World Set Free,' coining the term 'atomic bomb.'
The genre of steampunk imagines a world powered by steam technology, often set in a Victorian-era aesthetic.
Orson Scott Card's 'Ender's Game' was originally published as a short story in 1977 before being expanded into a novel in 1985.
Isaac Asimov was born in Petrovichi, Russia, and emigrated to the United States at the age of three.
Andy Weir self-published 'The Martian' on his website one chapter at a time, and it later became a bestselling novel.
The term 'robot' was introduced by Czech playwright Karel Čapek in his 1920 play 'R.U.R. (Rossum's Universal Robots).'
Ray Bradbury never learned to drive a car and instead relied on public transportation and his bicycle his entire life.
The first science fiction magazine, 'Amazing Stories,' was founded by Hugo Gernsback in April 1926.
A generation ship is a hypothetical starship designed to travel for centuries, with successive generations living and dying aboard.
Becky Chambers' 'The Long Way to a Small, Angry Planet' was initially self-published through a crowdfunding campaign.
George Orwell's '1984' introduced concepts like Big Brother, thoughtcrime, and Newspeak into popular culture.
The Philip K. Dick Award is given annually for the best original science fiction paperback published in the United States.
Arthur C. Clarke once said that the only way to discover the limits of the possible is to go beyond them into the impossible.
The concept of terraforming — transforming a planet's atmosphere to make it habitable — was coined by Jack Williamson in 1942.
Hugo Gernsback's 'Ralph 124C 41+' (1911) predicted television, radar, and synthetic fabrics.
The first WorldCon was held in New York City in 1939, attended by about 200 fans.
Octavia Butler was the first science fiction writer to receive a MacArthur Foundation 'Genius Grant.'
The 'grandfather paradox' is one of the most famous thought experiments in time travel theory.
Robert Heinlein is often called the 'Dean of Science Fiction' and was one of the first sci-fi authors to break into mainstream publishing.
Heavy Metal magazine, founded in 1977, became one of the most influential sci-fi and fantasy comics publications worldwide.
The concept of parallel universes in quantum mechanics inspired the 'many-worlds' interpretation popular in sci-fi.
Frank Herbert spent six years researching and writing 'Dune,' which drew on ecology, religion, and Middle Eastern culture.
The Arthur C. Clarke Award for best science fiction novel published in the UK has been given annually since 1987.
Mary Shelley's 'Frankenstein' (1818) is widely considered the first true science fiction novel.
NASA has cited Arthur C. Clarke and other science fiction authors as inspiring real-world space exploration programs.
Stanisław Lem's 'Solaris' explores the limits of human understanding when confronted with a truly alien intelligence.
The Three Laws of Robotics are: a robot may not injure a human, must obey humans, and must protect its own existence.
William Gibson wrote 'Neuromancer' on a manual typewriter, despite the novel being about advanced computer networks.
The concept of faster-than-light travel remains theoretical, with ideas like warp drives and wormholes explored in both physics and fiction.
Philip K. Dick's works have been adapted into numerous films, though many deviate significantly from his original stories.
The first Nebula Awards ceremony was held in 1966, honoring the best science fiction and fantasy published in 1965.
Solarpunk is a speculative fiction genre that envisions optimistic futures focused on renewable energy and sustainable communities.
Kurt Vonnegut's 'Slaughterhouse-Five' blends science fiction with anti-war themes based on his WWII experiences.
The space opera subgenre features epic adventures set in space, often involving galactic empires and interstellar conflict.
H.G. Wells published 'The War of the Worlds' in 1898, one of the earliest stories depicting an alien invasion of Earth.
Ursula K. Le Guin was the daughter of renowned anthropologist Alfred Kroeber, which influenced her explorations of alien cultures.
The first Hugo Award was presented at the 11th World Science Fiction Convention in Philadelphia in 1953.
In 1926, Hugo Gernsback described television-like devices he called 'telephots' in his fiction.
Ray Bradbury's 'The Martian Chronicles' was published in 1950 and depicted human colonization of Mars.
2000 AD is a British weekly sci-fi comic that launched in 1977 and introduced Judge Dredd.
The novum is a science fiction concept referring to the new or strange element that distinguishes the story from reality.
Isaac Asimov's Foundation series was originally inspired by Edward Gibbon's 'The History of the Decline and Fall of the Roman Empire.'
The concept of a ringworld — an artificial ring-shaped habitat encircling a star — was popularized by Larry Niven's 1970 novel.
Ted Chiang has published fewer than 20 stories in his career but has won multiple Hugo and Nebula Awards.
Arthur C. Clarke lived in Sri Lanka for most of his adult life, where he pursued his passion for scuba diving.
The 'uncanny valley' concept, where humanlike robots become unsettling, was first described by Masahiro Mori in 1970.
Jules Verne is the second most translated author in the world, after Agatha Christie.
Cyberpunk fiction typically features near-future settings with advanced technology, societal decay, and anti-establishment themes.
Douglas Adams wrote 'The Hitchhiker's Guide to the Galaxy' after lying drunk in a field in Innsbruck, Austria, staring at the stars.
The ansible, a device for instantaneous communication across any distance, was introduced by Ursula K. Le Guin in 'Rocannon's World.'
Robert Heinlein's 'Starship Troopers' sparked debates about militarism and citizenship that continue to this day.
Ghost in the Shell, created by Masamune Shirow, explored themes of consciousness and identity in a cyberpunk setting.
The Alcubierre drive is a theoretical concept proposed by physicist Miguel Alcubierre that could allow faster-than-light travel.
Fanzines — fan-made magazines — have been a cornerstone of sci-fi fandom since the 1930s.
Philip K. Dick was born in Chicago in 1928 and spent most of his life in California.
The 'Kardashev scale' classifies civilizations by their energy consumption: planetary, stellar, or galactic.
Samuel R. Delany's 'Dhalgren' remains one of the most controversial and enigmatic sci-fi novels ever published.
H.G. Wells anticipated aerial warfare in his 1908 novel 'The War in the Air.'
The concept of cryogenic suspension — freezing humans for future revival — is a staple of sci-fi that has some basis in real cryonics research.
Lois McMaster Bujold has won more Hugo Awards for Best Novel than any other author, with four wins.
Ray Bradbury was born in Waukegan, Illinois, in 1920, which he fictionalized as 'Green Town' in several novels.
The genre of hard science fiction emphasizes scientific accuracy and logical consistency in its worldbuilding.
Frank Herbert was born in Tacoma, Washington, in 1920.
In Arthur C. Clarke's '2001: A Space Odyssey,' astronauts use flat-screen tablet devices decades before iPads existed.
The O'Neill cylinder is a proposed space colony design consisting of two counter-rotating cylinders to simulate gravity.
Cosplay at science fiction conventions dates back to the first WorldCon in 1939, when Forrest J Ackerman wore a futuristic costume.
Ursula K. Le Guin was born in Berkeley, California, on October 21, 1929.
The concept of a 'singularity' — when AI surpasses human intelligence — was popularized by Vernor Vinge in a 1993 essay.
Brian Aldiss argued that Mary Shelley's 'Frankenstein' was the first true science fiction novel in his critical work 'Billion Year Spree.'
Isaac Asimov had claustrophilia — he actually enjoyed small, enclosed spaces.
The first science fiction book club, the Science Fiction Book Club, was founded in 1953.
Afrofuturism blends science fiction, African mythology, and Black culture to imagine alternative futures and histories.
Robert Heinlein described waterbeds in his 1961 novel 'Stranger in a Strange Land,' years before they were commercially produced.
A Matrioshka brain is a hypothetical megastructure of concentric Dyson spheres, each layer powered by the waste heat of the one inside it.
Greg Bear's 'Blood Music' explored nanotechnology and biological computing before those fields became mainstream.
H.G. Wells published 'The Invisible Man' in 1897, exploring the consequences of a scientist achieving invisibility.
The concept of first contact — humanity's initial encounter with alien life — is one of the most enduring themes in sci-fi.
Arthur C. Clarke passed away in Colombo, Sri Lanka, at the age of 90.
John W. Campbell edited 'Astounding Science Fiction' magazine and mentored many of the genre's greatest writers.
The 'Prime Directive' concept of non-interference with less advanced civilizations appears in many sci-fi universes beyond Star Trek.
Aldous Huxley and George Orwell presented contrasting dystopias: one of pleasure and control, the other of fear and surveillance.
The Clarke Award shortlist is chosen by a panel of judges representing the British Science Fiction Association and other organizations.
Jules Verne was born in Nantes, France, in 1828.
Robert Heinlein was the first science fiction writer to appear on the New York Times Best Seller list.
The Bussard ramjet is a theoretical spacecraft that collects interstellar hydrogen as fuel, allowing near-light-speed travel.
Iain M. Banks created the Culture series, depicting a post-scarcity utopian society managed by incredibly advanced AIs.
Liu Cixin's 'The Dark Forest' proposes a chilling solution to the Fermi Paradox: civilizations hide because detection means destruction.
Science fiction fandom was one of the first subcultures to develop a vibrant online community, starting with Usenet groups in the 1980s.
Kim Stanley Robinson's Mars trilogy — 'Red Mars,' 'Green Mars,' and 'Blue Mars' — provides a detailed vision of terraforming Mars.
The Turing test was originally called 'The Imitation Game' in Alan Turing's 1950 paper.
H.G. Wells was born in Bromley, Kent, England.
Solaris by Stanisław Lem suggests that truly alien intelligence may be fundamentally incomprehensible to humans.
Margaret Atwood has often debated whether her works like 'The Handmaid's Tale' are science fiction or speculative fiction.
The concept of uploading consciousness to a computer is explored extensively in sci-fi as 'mind uploading' or 'whole brain emulation.'
John Wyndham's 'The Day of the Triffids' depicted a post-apocalyptic world overrun by mobile, predatory plants.
Isaac Asimov died on April 6, 1992, from heart and kidney failure related to an earlier HIV infection from a blood transfusion.
Hard sci-fi author Hal Clement was known for creating aliens and worlds that strictly obeyed the laws of physics.
The concept of nanotechnology in fiction was heavily influenced by Eric Drexler's 1986 book 'Engines of Creation.'
Connie Willis has won more Hugo and Nebula Awards combined than any other science fiction author.
The 'generation ship' concept was explored in Robert Heinlein's 1941 novella 'Universe,' where passengers forget they are on a ship.
Biopunk is a subgenre focusing on biotechnology, genetic engineering, and the manipulation of living organisms.
Arthur C. Clarke was born in Minehead, Somerset, England.
The ansible concept has been adopted by many other authors and even lent its name to the IT automation tool Ansible.
James Tiptree Jr. was the pen name of Alice Sheldon, whose true identity remained hidden for years in the sci-fi community.
The concept of virtual reality in fiction predates the technology, appearing in Stanley Weinbaum's 1935 story 'Pygmalion's Spectacles.'
Fred Hoyle, a renowned astronomer, also wrote science fiction, including 'The Black Cloud' about an intelligent interstellar gas cloud.
The Fermi Paradox was informally articulated by physicist Enrico Fermi during a lunch conversation in 1950.
Octavia Butler's 'Kindred' combined time travel with the history of American slavery in a powerful literary fusion.
Robert Heinlein was born in Butler, Missouri.
The 'ansible' as a concept solves the light-speed communication barrier that would make interstellar governance impossible.
Philip K. Dick won the Hugo Award for Best Novel for 'The Man in the High Castle' in 1963.
The concept of a 'hive mind' — a collective consciousness shared by many individuals — is a common sci-fi trope.
Douglas Adams said the answer to life, the universe, and everything — 42 — was chosen because it was a completely ordinary number.
China Miéville blends science fiction with fantasy and Marxist theory in works like 'Perdido Street Station.'
The warp drive concept from science fiction was given theoretical grounding by physicist Miguel Alcubierre in 1994.
Sci-fi convention badges and name tags evolved into an art form, with elaborate ribbons and decorations.
Samuel Butler's 'Erewhon' (1872) was one of the earliest works to explore the idea of machines developing consciousness.
Ray Bradbury died on June 5, 2012, at the age of 91.
The 'Ringworld' concept was so influential that MIT students wrote a paper pointing out engineering flaws, which Niven later addressed in a sequel.
George Orwell's '1984' has seen surges in sales during periods of political controversy and surveillance debates.
The International Space Station, orbiting Earth since 1998, has realized many predictions from early science fiction.
Atompunk is a retro-futuristic subgenre inspired by the Atomic Age of the 1940s through 1960s.
Vernor Vinge's concept of the technological singularity predicts a point where superintelligent AI transforms civilization beyond human comprehension.
Le Guin's 'The Dispossessed' is subtitled 'An Ambiguous Utopia' and contrasts anarchist and capitalist societies.
The International Space Station has a library that includes science fiction books brought by astronauts.
Asimov's Foundation series uses 'psychohistory,' a fictional science that predicts the behavior of large populations mathematically.
Ann Leckie's 'Ancillary Justice' won the Hugo, Nebula, and Arthur C. Clarke Awards — a rare triple crown.
SpaceX CEO Elon Musk has cited Asimov's Foundation series and Douglas Adams as major influences on his vision for space exploration.
The moon landing on July 20, 1969, fulfilled predictions made by sci-fi writers for over a century.
Dieselpunk is an aesthetic and genre combining the interwar period (1920s-1940s) with retro-futuristic technology.
Becky Chambers' Wayfarers series is celebrated for its focus on character relationships and found family in a space opera setting.
Cordwainer Smith's sci-fi was deeply influenced by his real-life career as a psychological warfare expert.
Frank Herbert died on February 11, 1986, leaving the Dune saga unfinished.
Jack Vance's 'Dying Earth' series influenced the magic system in Dungeons & Dragons.
The concept of uplift — genetically engineering animals to human-level intelligence — was the central theme of David Brin's Uplift series.
Clarke's First Law states: 'When a distinguished scientist states that something is possible, he is almost certainly right.'
The Three-Body Problem trilogy by Liu Cixin has sold over 8 million copies worldwide.
Early science fiction was often published in pulp magazines, named for the cheap wood-pulp paper they were printed on.
NASA's Innovative Advanced Concepts program has funded research into ideas that were once purely science fiction.
The Voyager Golden Records, launched in 1977, carry sounds and images of Earth into interstellar space — a concept straight from sci-fi.
Alfred Bester's 'The Demolished Man' won the very first Hugo Award for Best Novel in 1953.
The concept of a 'world ship' is an even larger version of a generation ship, essentially a mobile space habitat the size of a small world.
Philip K. Dick's estate controls a large body of work, with over 44 published novels and approximately 121 short stories.
The 'novella' format — longer than a short story, shorter than a novel — has always been popular in science fiction publishing.
Ursula K. Le Guin died on January 22, 2018, at her home in Portland, Oregon.
The concept of antimatter, now confirmed by physics, was used in science fiction long before it was experimentally observed.
John Scalzi's 'Old Man's War' reimagined the military science fiction genre for a new generation of readers.
Hugo Gernsback founded Amazing Stories magazine in 1926, creating the first publication dedicated entirely to science fiction.
The panspermia hypothesis — that life on Earth originated from space — has been explored in many sci-fi works.
Harlan Ellison famously wrote 'I Have No Mouth, and I Must Scream' about a godlike AI tormenting the last humans on Earth.
The first Hugo Award ceremony at the 1953 WorldCon almost didn't happen because organizers worried nobody would attend.
Larry Niven's Ringworld has a surface area roughly three million times that of Earth.
The 'galaxy brain' is a concept in sci-fi where entire galaxies are converted into computing substrates.
Joanna Russ's 'The Female Man' was a landmark feminist science fiction novel published in 1975.
Mark Twain's 'A Connecticut Yankee in King Arthur's Court' (1889) is one of the earliest time travel novels.
The concept of a 'multiverse' has moved from science fiction into mainstream theoretical physics.
Ray Bradbury's 'Fahrenheit 451' takes its title from the supposed auto-ignition temperature of paper.
The Science Fiction Research Association (SFRA) has been promoting scholarly study of sci-fi since 1970.
Roger Zelazny's 'Lord of Light' blends Hindu mythology with far-future science fiction themes.
'Neuromancer' by William Gibson was published on July 1, 1984, and launched the cyberpunk movement.
The concept of a 'Von Neumann probe' — a self-replicating spacecraft — was proposed based on mathematician John von Neumann's work.
David Brin has worked as a NASA consultant in addition to his career as a science fiction author.
Asimov's 'I, Robot' is a collection of nine short stories, not a single novel, despite the common misconception.
The subgenre of 'mundane science fiction' restricts itself to scientifically plausible technologies and settings.
Joe Haldeman's 'The Forever War' used relativistic time dilation to comment on the Vietnam War experience.
The concept of a 'tractor beam' from sci-fi has been partially realized using focused beams of light to move microscopic particles.
N.K. Jemisin's Broken Earth trilogy begins with the end of the world in the very first sentence of 'The Fifth Season.'
The Hugos are voted on by members of the World Science Fiction Convention, making them a populist award.
Arthur C. Clarke's '2001: A Space Odyssey' was developed simultaneously as both a novel and a film.
Olaf Stapledon's 'Star Maker' (1937) depicted the entire history of the universe and influenced Clarke and others.
The 'zoo hypothesis' suggests that advanced aliens deliberately avoid contact with Earth, much like a wildlife preserve.
Doug Adams' 'The Hitchhiker's Guide to the Galaxy' began as a BBC Radio 4 comedy series in 1978.
Gene Wolfe's 'The Book of the New Sun' is set so far in the future that the sun is dying and technology is indistinguishable from magic.
Real-world earbuds were predicted by Ray Bradbury in 'Fahrenheit 451,' where he described 'thimble radios' worn in the ear.
The New Wave science fiction movement of the 1960s and 1970s emphasized literary style and social themes over hard science.
K. Eric Drexler's concept of molecular nanotechnology was popularized in Michael Crichton's 'Prey.'
Isaac Asimov's 'Foundation' was originally published as a series of short stories in Astounding Science Fiction starting in 1942.
The UK's Interplanetary Society, founded in 1933, has included many sci-fi authors among its members.
Cory Doctorow's fiction often explores digital rights, copyright reform, and surveillance culture.
The concept of a Boltzmann brain — a self-aware entity arising from random quantum fluctuations — straddles physics and sci-fi.
Andy Weir researched orbital mechanics, botany, and chemistry extensively while writing 'The Martian.'
The first printed science fiction story is often cited as Johannes Kepler's 'Somnium' (1634), about a trip to the Moon.
Stanisław Lem estimated he had written about 45,000 pages of fiction and non-fiction during his career.
E.E. 'Doc' Smith's Lensman series, begun in 1934, was among the first major space opera sagas.
The Clarke Award winner receives a prize equal to the current year in British pounds — for example, £2026 in 2026.
Paolo Bacigalupi's 'The Windup Girl' explored themes of genetic engineering, corporate control, and climate change.
H.G. Wells and Jules Verne, though contemporaries, had very different approaches: Verne emphasized technical accuracy while Wells focused on social commentary.
The science fiction 'Big Three' traditionally refers to Isaac Asimov, Arthur C. Clarke, and Robert Heinlein.
Yevgeny Zamyatin's 'We' (1924) was the first modern dystopian novel and influenced both Orwell and Huxley.
The concept of powered exoskeletons in fiction, from Heinlein's 'Starship Troopers,' has led to real military and medical exoskeleton development.
Le Guin's Earthsea books, while primarily fantasy, incorporate themes common to science fiction like ecology and anthropology.
The SETI Institute searches for signals from extraterrestrial civilizations, a pursuit inspired in part by science fiction.
Jack McDevitt's Alex Benedict series combines science fiction with archaeological mystery.
Orson Scott Card held a 'boot camp' for aspiring science fiction writers for many years.
The term 'robotics' was first used by Isaac Asimov in his 1941 story 'Liar!' — he thought it was already a word.
The concept of a 'replicator' — a device that can create any object from raw materials — has parallels in modern 3D printing.
J.G. Ballard pioneered the 'inner space' movement in sci-fi, focusing on psychological landscapes rather than outer space.
Dan Simmons' 'Hyperion' draws its structure from Geoffrey Chaucer's 'The Canterbury Tales.'
The first translation of a Chinese sci-fi novel to win a major Western award was Liu Cixin's 'The Three-Body Problem.'
The concept of a 'doomsday device' in fiction mirrors real Cold War fears about mutually assured destruction.
Pat Murphy's 'The City, Not Long After' explores a post-apocalyptic San Francisco ruled by artists.
George Orwell completed '1984' while severely ill with tuberculosis on the Scottish island of Jura.
The idea of satellites for communication was first proposed in fiction before Clarke formalized it scientifically.
Pamela Sargent edited 'Women of Wonder,' one of the first anthologies dedicated to science fiction by women.
The Philip K. Dick Award has been presented annually since 1983.
The 'post-apocalyptic' subgenre explores life after civilization-ending events like nuclear war, pandemics, or asteroid impacts.
Brian Aldiss coined the term 'cosy catastrophe' for post-apocalyptic stories where middle-class British life continues relatively comfortably.
Edgar Rice Burroughs' Barsoom series, beginning with 'A Princess of Mars' in 1912, helped popularize planetary romance.
The concept of 'grey goo' — self-replicating nanobots consuming all matter — was described by Eric Drexler as a potential nanotechnology risk.
Alfred Bester's 'The Stars My Destination' is considered one of the greatest sci-fi novels, blending revenge with teleportation.
Many submarine captains have cited Jules Verne's Captain Nemo as an inspiration for their careers.
The concept of closed timelike curves in general relativity provides a theoretical basis for time travel explored in sci-fi.
Martha Wells' Murderbot Diaries feature an antisocial security android who would rather watch TV than interact with humans.
Robert Silverberg was named a Grand Master by the Science Fiction and Fantasy Writers of America in 2004.
The 'space race' between the US and USSR was fueled in part by visions of space travel popularized by science fiction.
Peter Watts' 'Blindsight' asks whether consciousness is actually necessary for intelligence.
George Orwell was born Eric Arthur Blair on June 25, 1903, in Motihari, British India.
Cyberpunk's visual aesthetic — neon lights, rain-slicked streets, massive corporate towers — has influenced architecture and design.
The Nebula Awards are juried by fellow writers, unlike the Hugo Awards which are voted on by convention attendees.
Philip K. Dick wrote many of his novels while living in poverty in the San Francisco Bay Area.
The concept of 'unobtainium' — a fictional material with impossible properties — has become a tongue-in-cheek engineering term.
Robert Charles Wilson's 'Spin' posits that Earth has been enclosed in a membrane that slows time relative to the rest of the universe.
Frederik Pohl's 'Gateway' features an alien space station with ships pre-programmed for unknown destinations.
The first major fan convention for any genre was the first World Science Fiction Convention in 1939.
Ada Palmer's Terra Ignota series is set in a 25th century governed by philosophical principles rather than national borders.
Aldous Huxley was born on July 26, 1894, in Godalming, Surrey, England.
The Drake Equation was formulated by Frank Drake in 1961 to estimate the number of communicative civilizations in our galaxy.
Sci-fi author Spider Robinson won the Hugo Award with his Callahan's Crosstime Saloon stories about a bar where time travelers and aliens gather.
The concept of a holodeck — an immersive virtual reality room — is being approached by modern VR cave systems.
Greg Egan's hard sci-fi explores post-human existence and abstract mathematical concepts with rigorous scientific accuracy.
Arthur C. Clarke and Isaac Asimov had a friendly agreement: Clarke was the best science fiction writer and Asimov was the best science writer.
Liu Cixin was born in Beijing in 1963 and worked as a computer engineer at a power plant before becoming a full-time writer.
The term 'speculative fiction' is sometimes preferred over 'science fiction' as it encompasses a broader range of imaginative literature.
Charles Stross's Laundry Files series blends Lovecraftian horror with spy fiction and information technology.
The 1938 radio broadcast of 'The War of the Worlds' by Orson Welles reportedly caused panic among some listeners who believed it was real.
The concept of a 'sleeper ship' — where passengers are in suspended animation during interstellar travel — remains a theoretical possibility.
Susanna Clarke's 'Piranesi' won the Women's Prize for Fiction and blends elements of sci-fi with magical realism.
The concept of a megastructure — any artificial structure of enormous scale — is a staple of hard sci-fi worldbuilding.
Ted Chiang's 'Exhalation' won the Hugo, Nebula, and Locus Awards for Best Short Story.
The science fiction 'Golden Age' is generally considered to span from the late 1930s to the early 1950s.
Mary Shelley published 'Frankenstein' anonymously in 1818 when she was just 20 years old.
The concept of a stellar engine — a megastructure used to move entire star systems — was proposed by scientist Leonid Shkadov.
Octavia Butler was born on June 22, 1947, in Pasadena, California.
Neal Stephenson's 'The Diamond Age' envisioned nanotechnology-based interactive books that could educate and raise children.
Larry Niven created the concept of a 'Known Space' universe shared across many of his novels and stories.
The 'space western' subgenre combines frontier narratives with science fiction settings.
Lois McMaster Bujold's Vorkosigan Saga spans over 17 novels following the adventures of the physically disabled Miles Vorkosigan.
Early sci-fi predicted video calling; modern video conferencing technology now makes it an everyday reality.
Gene Roddenberry consulted with Isaac Asimov about making Star Trek more scientifically accurate.
The James Tiptree Jr. Literary Award, now called the Otherwise Award, recognizes sci-fi that explores gender.
The mass driver — an electromagnetic launcher for propelling objects into space — was first described in sci-fi before becoming a real engineering concept.
C.J. Cherryh's Alliance-Union universe spans over 30 novels covering thousands of years of human expansion into space.
The concept of panopticon surveillance from Jeremy Bentham has been adopted as a metaphor in dystopian sci-fi.
Alastair Reynolds, a former ESA astrophysicist, writes hard sci-fi informed by his scientific background.
Orson Scott Card's 'Speaker for the Dead' was actually the book he wanted to write first, creating 'Ender's Game' as a prequel.
The concept of 'generation loss' — information degrading as it's copied — appears in many sci-fi stories about digital consciousness.
The 'zoo hypothesis' was formally proposed by John A. Ball in 1973 as an explanation for the Fermi Paradox.
Kameron Hurley's 'The Light Brigade' uses teleportation technology as a metaphor for the dehumanization of soldiers.
Frank Herbert's Dune series has sold over 20 million copies worldwide, making it the best-selling science fiction novel of all time.
The concept of a 'torch ship' — a spacecraft with an incredibly efficient drive — was a staple of Heinlein's fiction.
Olaf Stapledon's 'Last and First Men' covers two billion years of human evolution across 18 distinct human species.
Isaac Asimov's 'Nightfall' was voted the best science fiction short story of all time by the Science Fiction Writers of America in 1964.
The New Weird movement, led by writers like China Miéville, blends sci-fi with horror and surrealism.
Real-world experiments with ion drives, used in NASA's Dawn spacecraft, bring sci-fi propulsion concepts closer to reality.
Philip K. Dick died on March 2, 1982, just months before the release of 'Blade Runner,' based on his novel.
The concept of a 'world brain' — a global information network — was described by H.G. Wells in 1938, anticipating the internet.
Vandana Singh writes science fiction informed by Indian culture and non-Western perspectives on technology and society.
Theodore Sturgeon's Law — '90% of everything is crud' — was originally stated in defense of science fiction's quality.
The Bernal sphere is a proposed space colony design featuring a spherical shell roughly 16 km in diameter.
Arkady and Boris Strugatsky were the most famous science fiction authors of the Soviet Union.
William Gibson's 'Neuromancer' won the Hugo, Nebula, and Philip K. Dick Awards — the sci-fi 'triple crown.'
Douglas Adams was born in Cambridge, England, on March 11, 1952.
The concept of solar sails — spacecraft propelled by radiation pressure from sunlight — was described in sci-fi decades before Japan's IKAROS proved it worked.
Robert Sawyer's novels often explore the intersection of paleontology, quantum physics, and consciousness.
The Locus Award, given by Locus Magazine, is one of the most prestigious prizes in science fiction and fantasy.
Nalo Hopkinson blends Caribbean folklore with science fiction in novels like 'Brown Girl in the Ring.'
The concept of asteroid mining, common in sci-fi, is now being seriously pursued by companies like Planetary Resources.
Samuel R. Delany's 'Babel-17' explored the idea that language can shape and even control thought.
Ray Bradbury refused to allow 'Fahrenheit 451' to be published as an e-book for many years, only relenting in 2011.
The Stanford torus is a space colony concept proposed in 1975 that could house 10,000 to 140,000 people.
Nancy Kress's 'Beggars in Spain' explores a world where genetic engineering creates humans who never need to sleep.
The idea of a 'clone army' in fiction predates real cloning technology by decades.
Kurt Vonnegut was reluctant to be labeled a science fiction writer, fearing it diminished critical respect for his work.
The first English-language sci-fi magazine published in Japan was 'Hayakawa's S-F Magazine,' starting in 1960.
John Brunner's 'Stand on Zanzibar' (1968) predicted overpopulation, terrorism, and Viagra-like drugs.
The concept of a megacity — a sprawling urban center home to tens of millions — is a fixture of cyberpunk fiction.
Jo Walton's 'Among Others' is a semi-autobiographical novel about a young woman whose life is shaped by reading science fiction.
Arthur C. Clarke proposed that future technology would be able to terraform Venus by seeding its atmosphere with algae.
The concept of a 'Bracewell probe' — an autonomous interstellar probe that communicates with other civilizations — was theorized in 1960.
Vonda McIntyre's 'Dreamsnake' won both the Hugo and Nebula Awards in 1979.
The Strugatsky brothers' 'Roadside Picnic' introduced the concept of an alien 'Zone' with incomprehensible artifacts.
Science fiction conventions typically feature panels, readings, art shows, masquerades, and dealer rooms.
Arkady Martine's 'A Memory Called Empire' explores how language and poetry can be tools of imperialism.
The first issue of Galaxy Science Fiction magazine, published in October 1950, featured a story by Isaac Asimov.
The concept of a 'memory palace' from classical rhetoric has been adopted in sci-fi for digital storage of experiences.
Ken Liu both writes original science fiction and translates Chinese sci-fi, including Liu Cixin's work, into English.
The concept of a 'generation starship' requires solving problems of ecology, governance, and culture for centuries-long voyages.
Heinlein's 'The Moon Is a Harsh Mistress' features a sentient computer named Mike and explores lunar colonist revolution.
The International Space Station has hosted a book club that has included science fiction selections.
The 'Kardashev scale' was proposed by Soviet astronomer Nikolai Kardashev in 1964 to classify advanced civilizations.
Adrian Tchaikovsky's 'Children of Time' depicts the evolution of spiders into an intelligent civilization.
A Penrose process extracts energy from a rotating black hole, a concept used in both theoretical physics and sci-fi.
R.A. Lafferty's surreal short stories are considered some of the most unique works in the science fiction canon.
Isaac Asimov coined the term 'psychohistory' for his Foundation series, blending psychology, history, and statistics.
The 'bootstrapping paradox' in time travel occurs when an object or information is sent back in time and becomes its own origin.
Mary Robinette Kowal's Lady Astronaut series imagines an alternative 1950s where a meteorite impact accelerates the space program.
The concept of a 'Shkadov thruster' — using a star's radiation pressure to move an entire solar system — was proposed in 1987.
Samuel Delany was one of the first openly gay African American science fiction writers to achieve mainstream success.
Kim Stanley Robinson's 'The Ministry for the Future' blends science fiction with climate activism and policy discussion.
NASA's Artemis program to return humans to the Moon echoes decades of sci-fi novels about lunar exploration.
The 'Fermi bubbles' — enormous structures extending from the Milky Way's center — have inspired speculative fiction about galactic engineering.
James S.A. Corey is actually the pen name of two authors, Daniel Abraham and Ty Franck, who co-wrote The Expanse series.
Kazuo Ishiguro's 'Never Let Me Go' uses science fiction elements to explore mortality and the ethics of cloning.
The 1968 novel 'Do Androids Dream of Electric Sheep?' by Philip K. Dick questions whether empathy is the defining human trait.
L. Ron Hubbard began his career as a science fiction writer before founding Scientology.
The subgenre of 'cli-fi' (climate fiction) focuses on the effects of climate change and environmental catastrophe.
Andy Weir's 'Project Hail Mary' features a protagonist who must save Earth by understanding alien biochemistry.
The concept of an 'orbital ring' — a ring of material orbiting a planet — could theoretically support space elevators around its circumference.
Ursula K. Le Guin refused the National Book Foundation's Medal for Distinguished Contribution to American Letters speech format and instead used it to advocate for genre fiction.
The concept of 'quantum entanglement' is often used (and misused) in sci-fi for instantaneous communication.
H.G. Wells' 'The Island of Doctor Moreau' explored genetic engineering and bioethics over a century before CRISPR.
The Retro Hugo Awards honor works from 50 or 75 years ago that were not eligible for Hugos at the time.
Nnedi Okofor's 'Binti' combines African Himba culture with far-future space travel.
The interstellar medium — the matter and radiation between star systems — poses real challenges for the interstellar travel depicted in sci-fi.
Isaac Asimov wrote his first published story, 'Marooned Off Vesta,' when he was just 18 years old.
The Worldcon Hugo ceremony has been held on every inhabited continent except Africa.
Tamsyn Muir's Locked Tomb series blends science fiction with necromancy and has a devoted online fandom.
The concept of 'computronium' — matter optimized for computation — is a common element in singularity fiction.
Robert Heinlein's novel 'Friday' predicted the rise of private courier services and balkanized nation-states.
The first Hugo Award for Best Short Story was given in 1955.
Charlie Jane Anders' 'All the Birds in the Sky' blends science fiction and fantasy, positing them as opposing worldviews.
A Bussard ramjet would theoretically use a magnetic field hundreds of kilometers wide to scoop up interstellar hydrogen.
The Japanese sci-fi tradition includes unique concepts like 'sekai-kei,' stories where personal relationships mirror global or cosmic stakes.
Robert Heinlein's 'The Door into Summer' (1957) described robotic household cleaners decades before the Roomba.
Connie Willis's time-travel novels set in Oxford, like 'Doomsday Book' and 'To Say Nothing of the Dog,' combine sci-fi with historical fiction.
The Nebula Award trophy is a block of Lucite with embedded rock crystal and a metallic spiral nebula.
Peter Hamilton's 'Night's Dawn Trilogy' totals over a million words across its three massive volumes.
The concept of AI alignment — ensuring artificial intelligence acts in humanity's interest — is now a serious research field inspired by sci-fi cautionary tales.
Vernor Vinge's 'A Fire Upon the Deep' introduced 'zones of thought,' regions of the galaxy where different physics applies.
LeVar Burton's Reading Rainbow featured science fiction books for young readers, helping introduce the genre to new audiences.
Douglas Adams died unexpectedly of a heart attack on May 11, 2001, at the age of 49.
The concept of a 'paperless office' predicted in sci-fi has only been partially realized, despite advances in digital technology.
N.K. Jemisin lives in Brooklyn, New York, and worked as a career counselor before becoming a full-time writer.
Jules Verne's 'Paris in the Twentieth Century,' written in 1863, predicted gas-powered automobiles, fax machines, and electric lighting.
The concept of 'uplifting' alien species to sentience raises ethical questions explored in David Brin's work and in real debates about animal cognition.
The Magazine of Fantasy & Science Fiction has been published continuously since 1949, making it one of the genre's longest-running publications.
Kurt Vonnegut's 'Cat's Cradle' introduced the fictional substance 'ice-nine,' which freezes all water it contacts.
The science fiction canon includes works originally written in dozens of languages, not just English.
Jeff VanderMeer's Southern Reach trilogy — beginning with 'Annihilation' — defined the 'New Weird' subgenre.
Elon Musk named SpaceX's autonomous drone ships after Culture ships from Iain M. Banks' novels.
The 'Kessler syndrome' — a cascade of space debris collisions — was explored in sci-fi before becoming a real concern for space agencies.
One of the largest sci-fi book collections in the world belongs to the Science Fiction and Fantasy Collection at the University of Liverpool.
Becky Chambers has described her Wayfarers books as 'hopepunk' — the opposite of grimdark fiction.
The first known use of the word 'astronaut' in fiction appeared in Neil R. Jones' 1931 story 'The Jameson Satellite.'
Le Guin's Hainish Cycle posits that humans were seeded on many planets by an ancient civilization called the Hain.
The concept of a 'generation gap' aboard generation ships is a frequent plot device in interstellar fiction.
Ted Chiang worked as a technical writer in the software industry while writing his award-winning fiction.
The 'dark forest' theory from Liu Cixin's work has become a widely discussed concept in real scientific circles studying the Fermi Paradox.
Robert Heinlein predicted the waterbed, the Cold War, and nuclear power in his early stories from the 1940s.
